records.title: Prior authorization rework loops
records.description: Clinics repeat documentation, payer portal checks, calls, and status follow-ups before treatment can proceed.
evidence: Repeated payer-specific forms
Manual status calls after portal submission
Treatment scheduling delayed while approvals are pending
current_process: Staff collect clinical notes
Authorization is submitted through payer-specific portals
Teams check status manually until approval or denial
records.opportunity_cost: Clinical capacity sits idle while staff spend time proving eligibility and medical necessity.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High across specialty practices and imaging centers
records.confidence_reasoning: Prior authorization burden is widely documented, but local cycle time and staff cost still require organization-specific measurement.
validation_notes: External evidence supports the friction pattern
Operational validation should compare authorization cycle time by payer
evidence_gaps: Payer-specific denial and approval timing
Internal labor minutes per authorization
solution_hypotheses: Standardize payer documentation checklists
Pre-fill authorization packets from structured clinical data

records.title: Insurance eligibility verification before each visit
records.description: Front desk teams repeatedly verify coverage, copays, deductibles, and plan changes using payer portals and calls.
evidence: Eligibility checks occur even for returning patients
Coverage can change between booking and visit
Portal data often needs staff interpretation
current_process: Staff open payer portals
Coverage details are checked manually
Information is copied into scheduling or billing systems
records.opportunity_cost: Administrative staff lose time that could be used for patient intake and scheduling throughput.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate to high in multi-payer outpatient settings
records.confidence_reasoning: Eligibility verification is common and measurable, but waste varies by payer mix and front desk workflow maturity.
validation_notes: Can be sampled from visit prep logs
Claim rejection patterns can confirm downstream impact
evidence_gaps: Average verification minutes per visit
Share of visits requiring repeated verification
solution_hypotheses: Batch verify coverage before appointment windows
Flag only coverage changes and exceptions for staff review

records.title: Claim denial triage and resubmission
records.description: Denied claims are reviewed, corrected, appealed, and resubmitted through fragmented workflows.
evidence: Denial codes require manual interpretation
Missing documentation is gathered after the fact
Resubmission deadlines create avoidable write-offs
current_process: Denials arrive in batches
Staff sort by payer and denial reason
Corrections or appeals are filed manually
records.opportunity_cost: Revenue is delayed or lost while teams spend labor on preventable correction cycles.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High for hospitals, physician groups, and ambulatory networks
records.confidence_reasoning: Denials and resubmissions leave structured traces in revenue cycle systems, making the constraint relatively defensible.
validation_notes: Denial reason codes support repeatable measurement
Appeal timing and recovery rate can be tracked
evidence_gaps: Preventable denial share
Labor cost per denial category
solution_hypotheses: Classify denials by preventability before assignment
Route recurring denial patterns back to front-end workflows

records.title: Referral leakage from manual handoffs
records.description: Specialty referrals are delayed or lost when faxed forms, call queues, and incomplete records interrupt the handoff.
evidence: Referrals depend on fax or phone follow-up
Missing records delay appointment creation
Patients often become the coordination layer
current_process: Primary care sends referral packet
Specialist office reviews documents
Scheduler contacts patient after eligibility and records checks
records.opportunity_cost: Care is delayed and specialist capacity may go unused because handoffs lack reliable status visibility.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High in specialty networks with heavy referral volume
records.confidence_reasoning: Referral leakage is a recognized handoff issue, but lost demand is harder to measure without closed-loop referral tracking.
validation_notes: Referral timestamps can expose handoff delay
Scheduled-versus-sent referral ratios can indicate leakage
evidence_gaps: Unscheduled referral count
Patient abandonment after referral
solution_hypotheses: Create closed-loop referral status tracking
Prioritize referrals missing required records

records.title: Post-discharge follow-up gaps
records.description: Hospitals and clinics struggle to confirm whether patients complete follow-up steps after discharge.
evidence: Discharge instructions are not always closed-loop
Primary care follow-up may be scheduled separately
Medication and home care coordination require repeated calls
current_process: Discharge instructions are generated
Care teams call patients manually
Follow-up completion is tracked unevenly
records.opportunity_cost: Preventable escalations can occur when handoffs are not verified early.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High for hospitals with complex discharge populations
records.confidence_reasoning: Care transition risk is well supported, though the administrative friction component needs local follow-up completion data.
validation_notes: Discharge and follow-up timestamps provide measurable signals
Readmission reviews can identify missed coordination
evidence_gaps: Follow-up completion rate by discharge type
Manual outreach attempts per patient
solution_hypotheses: Trigger follow-up tasks from discharge events
Track closed-loop completion by risk tier

records.title: Provider credentialing packet duplication
records.description: Provider credentialing teams repeatedly collect the same licenses, attestations, insurance documents, and work history.
evidence: Multiple payers request similar credentialing materials
Manual expiration tracking creates renewal work
Provider onboarding waits on document completion
current_process: Credentialing packet is assembled
Documents are submitted payer by payer
Renewal dates are tracked manually or semi-manually
records.opportunity_cost: Providers may be unable to bill or see covered patients while enrollment work repeats across payers.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate to high for growing provider groups
records.confidence_reasoning: Credentialing duplication is structurally visible, but impact depends on payer enrollment timing and provider start dates.
validation_notes: Packet completion dates can be audited
Enrollment lag can be compared across payers
evidence_gaps: Duplicate document requests per provider
Revenue delay while enrollment is pending
solution_hypotheses: Maintain reusable credentialing packets by payer
Alert on expiring documents before enrollment delays

records.title: Medication prior authorization exceptions
records.description: Medication approvals require payer-specific exception forms, clinical justification, and back-and-forth between pharmacy and prescriber.
evidence: Patients wait while exceptions are reviewed
Pharmacies contact prescribers for alternatives
Staff repeat similar justification language
current_process: Prescription is rejected or flagged
Pharmacy contacts prescriber
Clinical documentation is submitted for payer review
records.opportunity_cost: Patients may delay or abandon therapy while staff coordinate exception approval.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High in chronic disease and specialty medication workflows
records.confidence_reasoning: Medication authorization burden is well documented, while abandonment and clinical delay require pharmacy-prescriber matching.
validation_notes: Rejected prescriptions can be counted
Exception turnaround can be measured by medication class
evidence_gaps: Therapy abandonment after authorization delay
Prescriber staff time per exception
solution_hypotheses: Detect exception likelihood at prescribing time
Auto-assemble medication exception documentation

records.title: Release of information request backlog
records.description: Records teams process patient, legal, insurer, and provider requests with identity checks and format-specific delivery requirements.
evidence: Requests arrive through multiple channels
Identity and authorization checks remain manual
Records must be redacted, packaged, or transmitted securely
current_process: Request is received and validated
Relevant records are located
Staff prepare and transmit records
records.opportunity_cost: Administrative queues delay downstream care, claims, legal review, or patient access.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate in hospitals and large clinics
sources: HHS HIPAA right of access guidance
records.confidence_reasoning: Regulatory requirements and request workflows are clear, but backlog severity depends on request volume and fulfillment logs.
validation_notes: Request intake and completion timestamps are measurable
Request type mix can explain complexity
evidence_gaps: Average fulfillment time by request channel
Rework from invalid authorization forms
solution_hypotheses: Triage requests by completeness and urgency
Standardize authorization validation before queue assignment

records.title: Appointment call queue overflow
records.description: Patients call for scheduling, rescheduling, price questions, portal help, and follow-up instructions that could be routed more precisely.
evidence: High inbound volume mixes urgent and routine needs
Schedulers answer repeated availability questions
Missed calls become abandoned demand
current_process: Patient calls central or local scheduling line
Staff identify request type
Caller is scheduled, transferred, or asked to wait
records.opportunity_cost: Demand is delayed or lost while appointment capacity is unevenly exposed to patients.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate to high in multi-location outpatient networks
records.confidence_reasoning: Call center queues are easy to instrument, but attribution to lost access requires appointment and abandoned-call linkage.
validation_notes: Queue logs can show wait and abandonment
Scheduling data can show recovered demand
evidence_gaps: Caller intent distribution
Appointment slots lost after abandoned calls
solution_hypotheses: Route callers by intent before scheduler handoff
Expose routine scheduling paths through self-service

records.title: Manual quality measure abstraction
records.description: Teams extract, validate, and submit quality reporting data from clinical notes, registries, and EHR fields.
evidence: Measure definitions change over time
Structured fields may not capture the required context
Manual chart review fills reporting gaps
current_process: Reports identify missing measure data
Staff review charts
Results are corrected and submitted
records.opportunity_cost: Quality teams spend time proving performance instead of improving workflows that drive performance.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High for hospitals and value-based care groups
records.confidence_reasoning: Quality reporting requirements are formal and well documented, though abstraction labor varies by measure and EHR configuration.
validation_notes: Measure abstraction queues provide direct evidence
Manual chart review volume can be tracked over time
evidence_gaps: Labor hours per measure
Share of measures requiring manual chart review
solution_hypotheses: Map measures to structured data gaps before abstraction
Flag charts likely to require manual review

records.title: Patient statement confusion and support calls
records.description: Patients receive statements that are hard to connect to insurance explanations, visit details, and payment options.
evidence: Patients call to clarify balances
Insurance adjustments are difficult to interpret
Multiple statements can arrive for one episode of care
current_process: Claim is adjudicated
Patient balance is generated
Support staff explain charges after statements go out
records.opportunity_cost: Confusing bills delay payment and consume staff time that could be spent resolving higher-risk accounts.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate across outpatient and hospital billing operations
sources: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau medical billing research
records.confidence_reasoning: Patient billing confusion is well recognized, but linking statement design to support cost needs call reason and payment timing data.
validation_notes: Support call reason codes can validate confusion drivers
Payment timing can show collection delay
evidence_gaps: Statement-specific call volume
Balance resolution time after patient contact
solution_hypotheses: Classify confusing balances before statement release
Add account-specific explanations to patient billing flows

records.title: Preference card mismatch in procedural supply planning
records.description: Procedure supply lists drift from actual usage, causing over-picking, missing items, returns, and rushed substitutions.
evidence: Preference cards are updated inconsistently
Actual usage varies by clinician and site
Unused supplies are returned or wasted after setup
current_process: Supplies are picked from preference cards
Teams adjust during the procedure
Returns and substitutions are reconciled after the fact
records.opportunity_cost: Capital is tied up in excess inventory while procedural teams still face shortage risk.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High in procedural departments and surgical centers
records.confidence_reasoning: Preference card drift is operationally plausible and measurable, but waste estimates need item-level pick, use, and return data.
validation_notes: Case pick lists can be compared with actual usage
Return and waste logs can quantify excess supply
evidence_gaps: Unused item cost per procedure type
Frequency of urgent substitutions
solution_hypotheses: Compare preference cards against actual usage by procedure
Prioritize high-cost items with repeated return patterns

records.title: Duplicate demographic collection across intake channels
records.description: Patients repeatedly provide demographic and insurance details across portal forms, call center scripts, and front desk check-in.
evidence: Patients are asked for the same address, phone, insurance, and emergency contact data through multiple intake steps
Staff reconcile portal-submitted information with EHR registration fields
Mismatch correction often happens during the visit window
current_process: Patient completes portal or phone intake
Registration staff confirm and re-enter selected fields
Front desk resolves mismatches before visit completion
records.opportunity_cost: Patient access teams spend visit-window time reconciling basic data instead of clearing higher-risk registration exceptions.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate across outpatient networks with mixed digital and in-person intake
records.confidence_reasoning: The workflow pattern is common and locally measurable, but impact depends on registration system configuration and portal adoption.
validation_notes: Audit duplicate fields across portal, phone intake, and front desk workflows
Measure mismatch corrections per completed visit
evidence_gaps: Average re-entry minutes by visit type
Mismatch rate between patient-submitted and EHR registration data
solution_hypotheses: Promote one authoritative demographic profile across intake channels
Route only mismatched fields to registration staff

records.title: Coordination of benefits ambiguity before claim submission
records.description: Billing teams manually resolve primary and secondary payer order when patient coverage data is incomplete, stale, or contradictory.
evidence: Claims can reject when payer order is wrong
Staff contact patients or payers to clarify active coverage
Coverage order is often corrected after an initial failed billing attempt
current_process: Coverage is collected during registration
Billing staff review payer order when a claim exception appears
Corrected coverage is updated before resubmission
records.opportunity_cost: Cash collection slows while staff chase coverage order clarity that could be verified earlier.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate to high in organizations serving patients with multiple coverage sources
sources: Revenue cycle denial management benchmark pattern
records.confidence_reasoning: Claim exception and rejection data can validate the issue, but organizations need payer-specific reason code mapping.
validation_notes: Review claim rejections tied to coverage order
Compare eligibility checks against final claim payer sequence
evidence_gaps: Share of rejections caused by coordination of benefits
Average days delayed by coverage order corrections
solution_hypotheses: Flag conflicting payer order before claim submission
Create a coverage-order exception queue for multi-payer patients

records.title: Medical necessity documentation chase
records.description: Administrative teams chase clinicians for documentation required to support orders, claims, or payer medical necessity reviews.
evidence: Orders may lack payer-required supporting details
Staff send repeated messages to clinicians for missing documentation
Scheduling or billing waits until documentation is complete
current_process: Order or claim enters review
Staff identify missing medical necessity documentation
Clinician supplies addendum or supporting note
records.opportunity_cost: Care and reimbursement are delayed while high-value clinical time is redirected to documentation repair.
records.estimated_annual_impact: High in imaging, durable medical equipment, and specialty service workflows
sources: Administrative simplification and medical review workflow guidance
records.confidence_reasoning: The friction aligns with documented authorization and coverage review workflows, but measurement needs message and order status data.
validation_notes: Sample delayed orders for missing documentation reasons
Count clinician message loops before completion
evidence_gaps: Documentation chase volume by service line
Time from missing-document flag to completed addendum
solution_hypotheses: Detect missing payer-required documentation at order entry
Suggest documentation prompts before administrative review

records.title: Financial assistance screening after billing escalation
records.description: Patients who may qualify for financial assistance are often identified after statements, calls, or collections workflows have already started.
evidence: Assistance eligibility can be discovered late in the billing cycle
Support teams spend time explaining balances that may later be adjusted
Delayed screening can increase patient confusion and avoidable outreach
current_process: Patient balance is generated after adjudication
Statements and support calls reveal affordability issues
Financial assistance screening begins after patient contact
records.opportunity_cost: Organizations spend labor pursuing balances that may need earlier assistance routing.
records.estimated_annual_impact: Moderate in hospitals and clinics with high self-pay or underinsured populations
records.confidence_reasoning: Policy requirements and billing workflows support the concept, but validation requires account-level timing and assistance outcome data.
validation_notes: Track assistance approval after first statement date
Measure support contacts before assistance screening
evidence_gaps: Accounts eligible for assistance before statement generation
Avoidable outreach tied to late screening
solution_hypotheses: Screen likely assistance eligibility before first statement
Route eligible accounts to counseling before billing escalation
